---
title: "Brown bear abundance"
subtitle: |
  [NO_BAER_001]
indicatorID: 
  - NO_BAER_001
indicatorName: 
  - Brown bear abundance [Brunbjørnbestand]
verbatimName: 
  - Brunbjørnbestand
folderName: NO_BAER_001
country: Norway
continent: Europe
ECT: B3 Functional state characteristics
Realm: 
  - Terrestrial (T) # e.g. Freshwater (F)
Biome: 
  - T2 Temperate-boreal forests and woodlands biome
Ecosystem: 
  - T2.1 Boreal/montane forests
yearAdded: 2026 # the year when the indicator was first submitted to ecRxiv
yearLastUpdate: 2026
status: incomplete # options are complete, incomplete, or deprecated
Version: "1.0.0" # See wiki page: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/wiki#versioning
VersionComment: First version on ecRxiv; indicator described in the Norwegian Nature Index
url: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_BAER_001
Normalised: Yes
SpatialAggregationPathway: Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) - [Scale - Truncate] - Downscale (municipalities) - Aggregate (geographic region) - Aggregate (national level)
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
  docx:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
execute: 
  cache: true
author:
  - name: Aud H. Halbritter
    email: aud.halbritter@uib.no
    affiliations:
      - id: myID
        name: University of Bergen
AuthorList: Halbritter, A. H.
date: May 15, 2026
data_availability: gold
code_reproducibility: gold
open_science_badge: gold
callout-icon: false
lightbox: true
css: ../../../style.css
code-links:
      - text: Add a review
        icon: github
        href: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv
hide: FALSE # Change to FALSE
bibliography: references.bib
---

<!--# This is a template for how to document the indicator analyses. Make sure also to not change the order, or modify, the headers, unless you really need to. This is because it easier to read if all the indicators are presented using the same layout. If there is one header where you don't have anything to write, just leave the header as is, and don't write anything below it. If you are providing code, be careful to annotate and comment on every step in the analysis. Before starting it is recommended to fill in as much as you can in the metadata file. This file will populate the initial table in your output.-->

<!--# Load all you dependencies here -->

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gt)
library(here)
library(anybadger)
library(yaml)
library(tibble)
library(conflicted)
# weird workarounds for cache issues
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::lag)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
pull<-dplyr::pull
library(NIcalc)
library(ecTools)
library(MetBrewer)

# This workflow uses `renv`. Please run `renv::restore()` in order to reproduce the working environment and run the analysis.
# renv::restore()

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r source}
#| echo: false
#| cache: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```



<!--# The following parts are autogenerated. Do not edit. -->

::: {layout-ncol="6"}

```{r statusBadge}
#| echo: false
status_badge(as.character(st))
```

```{r versionBadge}
#| echo: false
version_badge(my_version_number = as.character(version[[1]]))
```

```{r dataBadge}
#| echo: false
data_badge(as.character(badges[[1]]))
```

```{r codeBadge}
#| echo: false
code_badge(as.character(badges[[2]]))
```

```{r openScienceBadge}
#| echo: false
open_science_badge(as.character(badges[[3]]))
```

```{r licenseBadge}
#| echo: false
# The following inserts a badge specifying the GPL GNU version 3 license. ecRxiv recommends using this license, but you can decide to use another. Contact ecRxiv for help in insert a badge for a different license
license_badge()
```


::: 
 

> **Recommended citation**: `r paste(auth, " ", year, ". ", name, " (ID(s): ", folder_name, ") ", "v. ", version, ". ecRxiv: ", url, sep="")`



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Metadata
```{r tbl-meta2}
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator metadata'
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
meta |>
  dplyr::filter(Variable %in% c(
    "Indicator ID",
    "Indicator Name",
    "Continent",
    "Country",
    "Ecosystem Condition Typology Class", 
    "Realm",
    "Biome",
    "Ecosystem",
    "Year added", 
    "Last update",
    "Version",
    "Version comment",
    "Normalised",
    "Spatial aggregation pathway")) |>   
  kable()

```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}

## Log

<!--# Update this logg with short messages for each update -->
- May 15, 2026 - Original PR
:::


<hr />

<!--# Document you work below.  -->

## 1. Summary

<!--# 
With a maximum of 300 words, describe the indicator in general terms as you would to a non-expert. Think of this as a kind of common language summary. Follow the four numbers sub-headers below. under point 2, it is possible under  include a bullet point list of the specific steps in the workflow. 
 -->

1. **Background and rationale**:
The brown bear (_Ursus arctos_) is a large omnivorous mammal found in forested and mountainous areas of Norway, mainly in the east, Trøndelag, and north.
As a top predator and scavenger it plays a functional role in boreal and montane forest ecosystems.
Population trends reflect habitat quality, connectivity, and anthropogenic pressures such as habitat loss, traffic mortality, conflict management, and licensed hunting.
Norway has long-running, DNA-based monitoring of brown bears, which provides a relatively solid basis for using the species as an indicator of ecosystem condition in relevant ecosystems.
The indicator is part of the Norwegian Nature Index and is documented on [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/brunbjoern).

2. **Methods**:
Estimated brown bear abundance (or an equivalent population index) is derived from the national DNA-monitoring programme and associated statistical models, not from harvest statistics.
Non-lethal genetic sampling (scats, hair, and other material) is used to identify individuals; population size is estimated and reported for large-carnivore management regions (_forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt_, regions 5–8 in Norway).
Values are normalised to a 0–1 indicator scale using reference levels (_X~100~_) defined for those management regions, with _X~0~_ representing the worst condition.
For Naturindeks reporting, scaled values are aggregated to five geographic regions and to the national level using area weights.

3. **Interpretation**:
An indicator value of 1 means that the brown bear population in the reporting unit is at least as large as expected under the reference condition.
Values decreasing toward zero indicate that the population index is further below the reference, reflecting negative change relative to the reference state.

4. **Results and development needs**:
The indicator is operational in Naturindeksen and can be used for reporting ecosystem condition where brown bear is a selected species indicator.

## 1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

1. **Bakgrunn og begrunnelse**: Brunbjørn (_Ursus arctos_) er et stort, altetende pattedyr som finnes i skog- og fjellområder i Norge, særlig i øst, Trøndelag og nord. Som rovdyr og åtseleter har arten en viktig funksjon i boreale og fjellnære skogøkosystemer. Bestandsutviklingen speiler habitatkvalitet, sammenheng i landskapet og menneskelig påvirkning, blant annet tap og fragmentering av habitat, trafikkdødelighet, skadeforvaltning og lisensjakt. Norge har langvarig, DNA-basert overvåking av brunbjørn [@broseth2024_bb], som gir et relativt godt grunnlag for å bruke arten som indikator på økologisk tilstand. Indikatoren inngår i Naturindeksen og er beskrevet på [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/brunbjoern).

2. **Metoder**: Estimert brunbjørnbestand (eller et tilsvarende bestandsindeks) bygger på det nasjonale DNA-overvåkingsprogrammet og tilhørende statistiske modeller, ikke på jaktstatistikk. Genetisk materiale (ekskrement, hår m.m.) brukes til å identifisere individer; bestandsstørrelse rapporteres for forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt. Verdiene normaliseres til en indikatorskala 0–1 med referanseverdier (_X~100~_) knyttet til disse regionene, og _X~0~_ som verste tilstand. For Naturindeks aggregeres skalerte verdier videre til fem geografiske regioner og nasjonalt med arealvekter.

3. **Tolkning**: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at brunbjørnbestanden i rapporteringsenheten er minst like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden. Verdier som nærmer seg null, viser at bestandsindekset ligger lenger under referansen og at tilstanden har utviklet seg negativt i forhold til referansen.

4. **Resultater og utviklingsbehov**: Indikatoren er operativ i Naturindeksen.


## 2. About the underlying data

The primary data source for this indicator is the national programme for DNA-based monitoring of brown bears in Norway, coordinated under the large carnivore monitoring framework and reported annually (e.g. [@broseth2024_bb]).
For Naturindeks reporting, prepared indicator objects (population estimates or indices with uncertainty, plus reference values) are obtained from the Naturindeks database or equivalent exports.

### 2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the assessment years in _Naturindeksen_: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.
To produce indicator estimates for these years, input from the DNA-monitoring programme is combined over a short window (typically two to three years), as specified in the Naturindeks indicator protocol.

The brown bear is tied to large-carnivore management regions (_forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt_).
Regional and national monitoring and management use regions 5–8 (Hedmark; Møre og Romsdal and Trøndelag; Nordland; Troms and Finnmark; see [Rovdata](https://rovdata.no/Brunbj%C3%B8rn/Bestandsstatus.aspx)).
In Naturindeksen, indicator and reference values are stored at this management-region scale (as IC units in the database).

### 2.2 Sampling design

Monitoring relies on opportunistic and targeted collection of DNA samples from the field (hunters, managers, researchers, and the public), followed by genetic analysis to assign samples to individuals and sexes.
Coverage is extensive but not a classical random plot design: detection depends on sample submission, field effort, and bear presence. The method is designed for capture–recapture–style inference on identified individuals rather than complete census. 
Biases can arise from uneven sampling intensity between areas and years, misidentified samples, and incomplete detection of individuals.
Full methodological detail is given on [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/brunbjoern) and in the annual monitoring reports [@broseth2024_bb].

### 2.3 Original units

At the monitoring stage, the core quantities are numbers of genetically identified individuals (and derived abundance or density estimates) from DNA profiles, reported nationally and by forvaltningsregion for rovvilt.
For Naturindeks, these are translated into indicator variables (expected values with uncertainty distributions) stored in the NI database before normalisation to 0–1.

### 2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Raw genetic monitoring results and annual reports should be cited from the NINA monitoring publications (e.g. [@broseth2024_bb]).
Methodological documentation for this species is linked from [Naturindeksen – brunbjørn](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/brunbjoern).

<!-- ### 2.5 Additional comments about the dataset -->

<!--# Optional. Text here -->

## 3. Indicator properties

### 3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B3 – Functional state characteristics. Brown bear is assigned to functional composition because population status reflects the presence and role of a large carnivore in the ecosystem (trophic regulation, carcass subsidy, and related ecological processes), not only the occurrence of a single species name in a species list.

<!-- 
### 3.1.1 Other condition typologies -->

<!--# Optional: Add text about other standards or typologies, such as alternative national condition typologies, and how the indicator relates to these -->


### 3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The ecosystem characteristic represented is a functionally intact large-carnivore component of boreal and montane forest ecosystems: an ecosystem where brown bear occurs at abundances consistent with the reference condition and can fulfil its ecological roles without being depressed far below sustainable levels.

### 3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator may covary with other large carnivore or mammal indicators in the same regions (e.g. wolf, wolverine, lynx) and with indicators linked to similar forest and mountain habitats.
Including several tightly correlated predator indicators in the same composite index without accounting for dependence could overweight the predator guild signal.

### 3.4 Impact factors

Important anthropogenic pressures include habitat alteration and fragmentation, infrastructure and traffic mortality, human–wildlife conflict and removals, licensed hunting and management actions, and climate-related changes affecting food resources and denning. 
Natural variation in reproduction and survival also affects the population index.

### 3.5 Spatial aggregation

Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) – [Scale – Truncate] – Downscale (municipalities) – Aggregate (geographic region) – Aggregate (national level): population estimates or indices are available per forvaltningsregion for rovvilt (IC unit in Naturindeksen). Each unit is normalised against _X~0~_ and _X~100~_ for that region (linear scaling with truncation to [0, 1]).

Because rovvilt regions and the five Naturindeks geographic regions are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, we use municipalities (`BSunits`) as a common basic spatial unit. We assign the (scaled) rovvilt-region value to each municipality belonging to that region (uniform-value assumption), hereafter referred to as downscaling. Forest area (`Skog`) is used as the aggregation weight because brown bear is a forest-associated species.

## 4. Reference condition and levels

### 4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition for brown bear in Naturindeksen is a boreal/montane forest ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the reference period used in the Norwegian Nature Index framework [@nybo_fagsystem_2017].

### 4.2 Reference levels

_X~100~_ is set per large-carnivore management region (IC unit in Naturindeksen), using suitable area and density within each _rovviltregion_ and expert assessment: it represents the expected brown bear population (or equivalent index) under reference conditions in that unit.

_X~0~_ is defined as no brown bears in the unit (functional absence).


#### 4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels and indicator values apply at the scale of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (IC units).
Aggregated results for the five Naturindeks geographic regions and for Norway are summaries of those units with area weights.

## 5. Uncertainties
### 5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated).
Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt, so they inherit model uncertainty at management-region scale and any mismatch between administrative regions and ecological variation within the five Naturindeks geographic regions.
Monitoring design and sample effort differ between species and regions; uneven coverage can affect the population models that enter Naturindeksen.

### 5.2 Reported uncertainties

The intervals in @tbl-baer-results, @fig-baer-norm, and @fig-baer-timeSeries are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for management-region abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each management region and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for the population index (on the variable scale before scaling).
We draw `nsim = 1000` values per region, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the regional reference level and linear scaling, then downscale by assigning each scaled draw to every municipality in that region and aggregate with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`: first from municipalities (`BSunits`) to each NI geographic region (`part`), then from regions to Norway, using forest area (`Skog`) as the weight.
The 1000 `sampled_mean` values per region and year describe inferential uncertainty for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator; we summarise them with the median and 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles as an approximate interquartile range.


## 6. References

::: {#refs}
:::

## 7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs `NIcalc` from GitHub `ChloeRN/NIcalc`, branch `include_ReadOnly_access` (see `renv.lock` and `install_deps.R`); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need `username` / `password` arguments here. When [NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59](https://github.com/NINAnor/NIcalc/pull/59) is merged into `NINAnor/NIcalc`, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile.
We save the RDS export under `data/` so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

```{r baer-import-api}
#| code-summary: "Download brown bear data from Naturindeks API"
#| eval: false

baerImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Brunbjørn"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(baerImport, here::here("data/baerImport.rds"))
```

```{r baer-load-import}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
#| code-summary: "Load saved Naturindeks import"

baerImport <- readRDS(here::here("data/baerImport.rds"))
```


## 8. Spatial units

The eight forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (rovvilt regions 1–8) are the IC units in Naturindeks for this indicator.
The five Naturindeks geographic regions (east, south, west, central, north; codes E, S, W, C, N) are taken from the `part` field on municipality-level `BSunits` in the same import, aggregated up to each management region (see Section 9).
Mainland Norway is the ecosystem accounting area.

## 9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at large-carnivore management-region scale (IC unit) with linear scaling, then downscale by assigning the scaled values to the municipalities in each region and propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with forest-area weights using `ecTools::ec_upscale()`.
At geographic (NI part) and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is a forest-area-weighted mean across municipalities (then, for Norway, one draw per NI region).
We use linear scaling and forest area (`Skog`) from the Naturindeks spatial framework as the weight.

Management-region population indices in the database are stored as predictive distributions.
Reference levels per region (_X~0~_ = 0, _X~100~_ from the download) are applied before any geographic or national aggregation.
We ignore uncertainty in the reference levels themselves.

### Assemble import and indicator object

`assembleNiObject()` reshapes `baerImport` so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that underlying spatial units (`BSunits`) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

```{r baer-assemble}
#| code-summary: "Assemble brown bear NI object (thematic, predefined regions)"
baerAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = baerImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)
```

### Reference levels (X~0~ and X~100~)

Before scaling, we read regional reference levels from `referenceValues` in the RDS export (rows with `yearId == 0` or reference year name).
For this indicator, _X~0~_ is zero individuals in the unit and _X~100~_ is the expert reference population index per rovvilt region (`expectedValue` in the reference slice).

```{r baer-refs}
#| code-summary: "Management-region reference levels from downloaded Naturindeks data"
#| tbl-cap: "Management-region reference levels (X0 = 0, X100) by rovvilt region (IC unit)."
refs <- baerImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue) |>
  dplyr::mutate(x0 = 0)
refs |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Resample non-scaled management-region variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores one value per rovvilt region as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution).
We draw `nsim = 1000` random values per region with `NIcalc::sampleObsMat()`.
Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type `tradObs`; rows with only a custom distribution use `customObs`.

Assessment years are 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.
The result `mgmt_draws` is a long table of simulated population indices (`value`) by `ICunitId` and `year`, not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale.

```{r baer-vars}
#| code-summary: "Observation types for sampleObsMat"
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
```

```{r baer-draw}
#| code-summary: "Resample management-region variables (non-scaled)"
#| tbl-cap: "Preview of resampled management-region variable values (first rows)."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mgmt_draws <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = baerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(mgmt_draws) |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Scaling to indicator values (linear)

Normalisation is done at management-region scale before any geographic or national aggregation.
Each draw is joined to its regional `x0` and `x100`, then mapped to `[0, 1]` with `ecTools::ec_normalise()` using linear increasing scaling from _X~0~_ to _X~100~_ (no upper-tail unimodal anchor).

```{r baer-join-scale}
#| code-summary: "Join reference levels and linear scaling to indicator scale"
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  left_join(
    refs |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)),
    by = join_by(ICunitId)
  )

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = mgmt_draws$value,
  x0 = mgmt_draws$x0,
  x100 = mgmt_draws$x100,
  fun = "linear"
)

mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |> add_column(indicator = ind)
```

The scatter plot below (@fig-baer-norm) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each rovvilt region (one facet per `ICunitName`). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

```{r baer-normplot}
#| eval: true
#| code-summary: "Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English)"
bear_plot <- ec_norm_plot(mgmt_draws$value, mgmt_draws$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ mgmt_draws$ICunitName)
ggsave(filename = here::here("img/baer_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = bear_plot, scale = 1.5)
```

```{r fig-baer-norm}
#| fig-cap: "Linear normalisation of brown bear population index to indicator values (one panel per management region)."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/baer_norm_plot_eng.png"))
```

### Aggregation weights (forest area by municipality)

Spatial aggregation uses forest area (`Skog`) on each underlying municipality in `BSunits`.
Municipalities are linked to rovvilt regions via `ICunits`, and each `BSunit` carries a Naturindeks geographic region code (`part`: E, S, W, C, N).
Rovvilt regions and NI parts are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, so we keep the data at municipality level once it is scaled: each municipality inherits the scaled indicator value of its rovvilt region (uniform-value assumption), and is then aggregated upward with its own forest area.

```{r baer-weights}
#| code-summary: "Forest area, NI part, and IC link per municipality"
mgmt_weights <- as_tibble(baerAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- baerImport$ICunits |>
  distinct(ICunitId = as.character(id), BSunitId)

mgmt_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))
```

Downscale the scaled indicator values from each rovvilt region by assigning them to every municipality in that region.
The result is a long table with one row per (`year`, `sim_id`, `BSunitId`) carrying its rovvilt region's indicator draw, its `part`, and the municipality's `Skog`.

```{r baer-downscale}
#| code-summary: "Downscale rovvilt-region draws to municipalities"
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

bsunit_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  right_join(
    mgmt_weights |> select(BSunitId, ICunitId, part, Skog),
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )
```

### Spatial aggregation

Aggregate from municipalities to NI geographic regions with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`, using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per municipality per replicate, forest-area-weighted mean).

```{r baer-aggregate1}
#| code-summary: "Municipality to NI part MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = bsunit_draws,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Skog,
  start_units = BSunitId,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/baer_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))
```

```{r baer-load-mc-regional}
#| include: false
mc_propagated <- readRDS(here::here("data/baer_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))
head(mc_propagated)
```

For the national indicator, propagate again from regions to Norway.
Summed forest area per geographic region is used as the weight at this step.

```{r baer-region-weights}
#| code-summary: "Total forest area per NI region"
region_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$Skog, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
region_weights
```

```{r baer-agg-national}
#| code-summary: "NI region to Norway MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated_for_national <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(region_weights, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated_for_national,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated_national, here::here("data/baer_mc_propagated_national.rds"))
```

```{r baer-load-mc-national}
#| include: false
mc_propagated_national <- readRDS(here::here("data/baer_mc_propagated_national.rds"))
head(mc_propagated_national)
```

Regional and national MC draws are combined for summarisation and export.

```{r baer-mc-combined}
#| code-summary: "Bind regional and national MC outputs"
mc_propagated_combined <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated_national)
```

## 10. Results

From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in `mc_propagated_combined`, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive spread between management regions).
The table (@tbl-baer-results) and time series (@fig-baer-timeSeries) summarise regional and national (`Norway`) values.

```{r baer-part-labels}
#| include: false
baer_part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)
baer_region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")
```

```{r baer-summaryStats}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Brown bear indicator summaries by region and year (inferential uncertainty for area-weighted means)."
summary_out <- mc_propagated_combined |>
  mutate(part = dplyr::recode(part, !!!baer_part_labels)) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(part = factor(part, levels = baer_region_levels)) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Brown bear abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_BAER_001"
  )
```

```{r fig-baer-timeSeries}
#| cache: false
#| fig-cap: "Indicator values for brown bear abundance by region and nationally, with uncertainty bands from the resampled distribution."
baer_plot_years <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
baer_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(baer_region_levels)),
  baer_region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = baer_plot_years),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region")
  )

y_hi <- max(plot_data$p_975, na.rm = TRUE)
y_max <- min(1, max(0.08, y_hi * 1.12, na.rm = TRUE))
y_breaks <- pretty(c(0, y_max), n = 6)

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = baer_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, y_max),
    breaks = y_breaks,
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.04))
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
```

```{r tbl-baer-results}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for brown bear abundance."

summary_out |>
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()
```

## 11. Export file

Regional and national Monte Carlo draws are exported in one table (`mc_propagated_combined`: `year`, `part`, `sampled_mean`; `part` includes NI regions and `Norway`).

```{r baer-export}
#| eval: false
#| code-summary: "Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws"
write.csv(
  mc_propagated_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_BAER_001_v1.0.0.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)
```

## 12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on _Naturindeks_ [@nater_naturindeks_2025].

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Session Info
<!-- You can leave this last part as it is, unless you have a good reason to alter it. -->

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html.
Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.  

```{r sessionInfo}
#| echo: false
sessionInfo()
```

:::
